sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Bradykinin (BK) and its primary receptor, the bradykinin 2 receptor (B2R), play pivotal roles in regulating oxidative stress. While B2R activation has traditionally been linked to elevated reactive oxygen species (ROS) and pro-apoptotic signalling, growing evidence indicates that, under specific contexts, it also exerts antioxidative and cytoprotective effects, enhancing cell survival, promoting proliferation and attenuating apoptosis.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We retrieved data from electronic databases PubMed, Web of Science, and ScienceDirect to systematically evaluate the bidirectional redox regulation mediated by B2R and propose a dynamic model that reconcile this apparent paradox.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Our analysis underscores a tight association between the oxidative outcome of BK–B2R signalling and the duration of stimulation: multiple independent studies, together with our time-stratified analyses, suggest that prolonged BK exposure is more likely to elicit antioxidative responses. Mechanistically, an early, transient rise in ROS may activate cascades such as mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK), subsequently inducing adaptive antioxidant defences (e.g. catalase upregulation), thereby reframing ‘controlled’ ROS not as a purely toxic byproduct but as a signalling cue that sustains redox homeostasis.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In sum, B2R is not a unidirectional pro-oxidant or antioxidant factor; rather, it functions as a context- and time-dependent modulator of oxidative stress. This refined understanding provides a conceptual framework for stage- or condition-specific B2R-targeted interventions in cardiovascular, neurological and inflammation-related disorders.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Oxidative stress is a pathological mechanism characterized by excessive production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the body, resulting in an imbalance between oxidation and reduction reactions. It is implicated in aging and the pathogenesis of many diseases [1,2]. Accordingly, elucidating how key mediators govern ROS generation and clearance is essential for understanding disease mechanisms and developing targeted interventions.

Bradykinin (BK) is often regarded as an inflammatory factor that can induce microvascular dilation, resulting in the extravasation of inflammatory cells and inflammatory mediators into the plasma, leading to local manifestations of redness, swelling, heat and pain [3]. Moreover, it is closely associated with the plasma renin–angiotensin system (RAS) and plays a regulatory role in blood pressure regulation in the cardiovascular system [4]. Building on this, BK acts predominantly through the bradykinin 2 receptor (B2R), engaging diverse signalling axes that may exert bidirectional effects on redox homeostasis.

In BK-mediated forms of angioedema – including acquired C1 inhibitor deficiency and ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema – excess BK activates the B2R, leading to increased vascular permeability in these subtypes, B2R antagonism has shown clinical benefit [5]. This clinical observation suggests that precise modulation of B2R activity not only shapes inflammatory and vascular responses but may also influence oxidative stress.

Prior studies indicate that BK–B2R, under certain conditions, can couple to Gαq/12–13 signalling, promote calcium influx, and activate nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) oxidases (e.g. NOX2/NOX4), thereby driving excessive ROS production, triggering oxidative stress, and exacerbating inflammation or even apoptosis [6,7]. However, accumulating evidence also shows that, in distinct temporal windows or tissue/cellular contexts, B2R can engage antioxidant pathways – including endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS), phosphoinositide 3-kinase (PI3K)–Akt and nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2) – increasing nitric oxide (NO) bioavailability and upregulating cytoprotective enzymes such as HO‑1 and superoxide dismutase (SOD), thus limiting oxidative injury [8]. This paradoxical, context‑dependent duality suggests that the pro‑oxidant versus antioxidant effects of B2R on ROS are governed by multiple determinants, including ligand concentration and exposure duration, receptor desensitization/internalization, crosstalk with other receptors (e.g. AT1R/AT2R of the RAS), the intrinsic cellular redox state, and disease‑specific microenvironments.

To systematically synthesize current evidence, we retrieved data from electronic databases PubMed, Web of Science, and ScienceDirect (from database inception to the present date). The following keywords were combined using Boolean operators AND/OR: BK, kininogen (KNG), kallikrein, BK receptor, ROS and oxidative stress. All resulting titles were manually screened, and abstracts matching the subject were reviewed. Full texts of abstracts of interest were accessed. References cited in full-text articles were also manually screened.

This review objectively describes the role of BK and B2R in oxidative stress and delves into the controversial relationship between them. It is believed that in the future, specific targeting and modulation of B2R can be used as a therapeutic approach for treating oxidative stress-related diseases.

sec.title: Generation and structure of bradykinin
sec.p: The earliest review found in PubMed regarding BK describes a study conducted by Frey et al. (1928), where they discovered that intravenous injection of pancreatic extract induced a hypotensive response in dogs [9]. They believed this was due to the action of a peptidase on plasma proteins, resulting in the release of an active peptide, which they named kallidin [9]. Subsequently, the research group led by Elliott et al. incubated bovine haemoglobin with pancreatic enzymes, leading to the release of a substance that caused hypotension and slow intestinal contractions in guinea pigs. This substance was named BK, and it was successfully isolated and purified, and the amino acids constituting it were identified [10]. In 1960, Boissonnas et al. synthesized a nonapeptide, Arg-Pro-Pro-Gly-Phe-Ser-Pro-Phe-Arg, which possessed biological, biochemical and biophysical properties similar to BK [11].

BK is not encoded by a dedicated gene; rather, it is liberated from KNG precursors encoded by the human KNG locus. Through alternative splicing, KNG produces transcripts for high-molecular-weight KNG (HK, encoded by KNG1) and low-molecular-weight KNG (LK). HK and LK share an identical heavy chain but differ in their C‑terminal light chains due to exon usage, which confers distinct binding and functional properties within the contact system. Upon proteolysis by plasma kallikrein and related kinin-releasing enzymes, BK and related bioactive peptides are released from these precursors [12]. This ensemble – comprising KNG, kallikreins, kinins and the bradykinin receptors (B2R/B1R) – is known as the kallikrein–kinin system (KKS). In humans, the KNG gene is the only gene encoding KNG and is confirmed through human–hamster cell hybridization to be located between the q26 and qter regions of chromosome 3 [13]. On the other hand, rats have two coding genes for KNG, namely KNG and T-KNG, which are located between the markers D11Mgh1 (VCATOMT) and D11Mgh3 (SATELLA) on chromosome 11 [14]. The mouse genome contains two homologous KNG genes, mKng1 and mKng2, which are closely located in a head-to-tail arrangement on chromosome 16 [15]. HK mRNA derived from mKng1 is primarily expressed in the liver and adrenal glands, responsible for the production of plasma KNG. On the other hand, HK mRNA derived from mKng2 is mainly expressed in the kidneys, but its translation quantity is insufficient to affect the level of plasma KNG [16,17].

In the human body, the expression of the KNG gene produces both LK and HK. These are secretory proteins and are predominantly produced in the liver, with limited expression in other tissues such as the brain, thymus, heart, lungs, spleen, kidneys, pancreas, placenta, colon, small intestine and testes. Kinin generation depends on compartment and enzyme specificity. In the plasma contact system, plasma prekallikrein bound to HK is activated and cleaves HK to release BK. In contrast, in tissues, members of the tissue kallikrein family preferentially process LK to produce Lys‑bradykinin (kallidin). The N‑terminal Lys of kallidin can be removed by aminopeptidases, yielding BK. Both BK and kallidin signal primarily via B2 receptors, whereas des‑Arg derivatives formed by carboxypeptidases signal via B1 receptors [18,19].

sec.title: Oxidative stress
sec.p: Life activities cannot be separated from oxidative stress. In 1957, Mills discovered glutathione peroxidase (GPx) while studying haemoglobin degradation metabolism. It is an enzyme in red blood cells that protects haemoglobin from oxidation and decomposition [20]. Later in 1970, Sies H demonstrated the presence of H2O2 in eukaryotic cells [21]. The total concentration of H2O2 in liver cells is estimated to be 10 nM, with a physiological range of 1–100 nM [22]. Since then, scholars have conducted extensive research on oxidative stress, which has been defined as an imbalance favouring oxidants over antioxidants, leading to redox signalling, control and/or molecular damage [23].

Oxidative stress includes both normal and pathological oxidative stress [24]. ROS can originate from all organelles, cells and tissues. Enzymes such as catalase (CAT), GPxs and peroxiredoxins (Prxs) maintain the level of ROS at a low physiological concentration by reducing them [25]. The generated signalling molecules are closely involved in metabolism (mitochondrial respiration), phosphorylation cascades, transcriptional regulation, cellular rearrangement, cell replication and other crucial cellular functions [26]. They can regulate the organism’s growth, immune response, energy metabolism, hormone secretion, cognition, tumour development and natural aging [27] and are indispensable in the physiological regulation of the organism [28].

However, when the body is exposed to poor nutrition, toxic substances, and ultraviolet or ionizing radiation, it can lead to excessive production of ROS within the body. Examples of these species include H2O2, superoxide anions, reactive nitrogen, sulphur, carbon, selenium and halogens. This imbalance in the redox reactions between mitochondria, peroxisomes, endoplasmic reticulum and the nucleus among other cellular components results in the oxidation of proteins, lipids, DNA and carbohydrates, compromising their physiological functions. This loss of compensation in redox reactions leads to a cascade of secondary oxidative stress, ultimately resulting in aging, diseases and even death. For example, excessive ROS from excessive exposure to ultraviolet radiation can cause skin aging [29]. Exposure to heavy metals and polluted environments can induce DNA damage caused by excessive ROS, promoting the development of tumours. Actively regulating the levels of ROS or enhancing antioxidant capacity can be used to treat diseases and maintain overall health.

sec.title: Kininogen and oxidative stress
sec.p: After the action of kininogenase, KNG releases HKa, low molecular weight kininogen (LMWK) and BK, which collectively play a role in the body [18]. In terms of oxidative stress, they seem to have detrimental pathological effects. Research indicates that serum levels of KNG increase during the aging process in rats and humans [30,31]. Furthermore, studies have shown that the KNG gene is upregulated in the doxorubicin (DOX)-induced oxidative stress model of myocardial cells, suggesting that overexpression of KNG can exacerbate cardiac oxidative stress and mitochondrial damage. Knocking down KNG can improve oxidative stress and mitochondrial damage caused by DOX [32]. Treating cells with lysed HK increases intracellular ROS production, activates the c-Jun N-terminal kinase (JNK)/forkhead Box O4 (FOXO4)/manganese superoxide dismutase (MnSOD) pathway, phosphorylates p38 kinase to inhibit telomerase activity, and promotes cell clone formation and proliferation, thereby inducing cell senescence and accelerating organ aging [33,34].

Certainly, KNG is not only closely related to oxidative stress but also demonstrates a close association with inflammation. In a model of adjuvant-induced arthritis in rats, an increase in levels of T-KNG can lead to elevated plasma KNG levels, indicating a strong correlation between this kinin precursor and inflammation [35,36]. A clinical study enrolled 15 female patients with chronic widespread pain (CWP) and 23 healthy female controls. Analysis of differential proteins in plasma between the two groups revealed that plasma KNG1 levels correlated with pain intensity [37,38]. Simultaneously, elevated levels of plasma kallikrein and BK were detected in the synovial fluid and plasma of patients with rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis, and these levels were positively correlated with the severity of joint pain and inflammation [39]. These pain sensations may be triggered by the release of BK following the cleavage of KNG-1 and induction of prostaglandin production [40].

sec.title: Kallikrein and oxidative stress
sec.p: Tissue kallikrein exhibits a wide spectrum of biological activity in multiple systems of the body, including the cardiovascular, renal and central nervous systems [41]. By overexpressing genes, adding proteins, and using stem cells as carriers, tissue kallikrein can be increased to stimulate cells such as renal cells, cardiomyocytes and glial cells. This process regulates the expression of oxidants and antioxidants in cells, as well as the phosphorylation activity of intracellular proteins [42]. It can significantly reduce the production of intracellular ROS, inhibit cell oxidative stress, decrease cell apoptosis, aging, inflammation, fibrosis, and promote the formation of new blood vessels, thus protecting against kidney, cardiovascular and neurological damage [42–44]. The reason why tissue kallikrein produces these effects may be due to an increased amount of tissue kallikrein cleaving KNG to generate sufficient BK to exert its actions, as this protective effect can be blocked by BK receptor antagonists or receptor knockout [45,46]. Conversely, clinical and in vivo studies confirm that serum and renal kallistatin levels are significantly elevated in patients with diabetic nephropathy (DN) and diabetic animal models. Overexpression of kallistatin exacerbates inflammation and oxidative stress levels, thereby worsening renal injury [47,48]. In vivo studies indicate that over expression of kallistatin effectively reduces serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen levels in diabetic mice, improves glomerular sclerosis and tubulointerstitial injury, and alleviates renal fibrosis. Concurrently, it diminishes aortic injury, aging, oxidative stress and inflammation in animal models, consistent with the actions of tissue kallikrein mentioned earlier [49–51]. This may be related to kallistatin having two functional domains – an active site and a heparin-binding site, leading to contradictory effects observed [52]. Below, we reviewed the role of tissue kallikrein in oxidative stress from the aspects of disease models, intervention methods and molecular mechanisms (Table 1).

sec.title: Bradykinin–bradykinin receptors and oxidative stress
sec.p: The preceding section summarized the beneficial protective effects of tissue kallikreins in most cells and tissues; mechanistically, most of the evidence indicates that tissue kallikreins cleave KNG and, through a series of processes, produce BK, which exerts its effects via the B2R.

We know that the BK receptors are G protein-coupled receptors, consisting of two types, bradykinin 1 receptor (B1R) and B2R. B1R is almost absent in healthy tissue but can be induced during inflammation, activating the nuclear factor-κB (NF-κB) signalling pathway, which forms a detrimental positive feedback loop in inflammation, exacerbating inflammatory pain and prolonging chronic inflammatory pain. Recently, in vivo studies indicate that diabetic rat retinas exhibit elevated expression of inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS), B1R, inflammatory cytokines (interleukin-1beta (IL-1β), tumour necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α)), vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF-A and its receptor VEGF-R2) and nitrosylated proteins, along with increased vascular permeability. These abnormalities can be reversed by the iNOS inhibitor 1400W [71]. In HEK293 cells, it has been shown that B1R can activate iNOS through extracellular signal-related kinases (ERKs) [72,73]. As a result, NO is generated, which can react with superoxide anions to produce peroxynitrite, a highly toxic molecule that leads to apoptosis of endothelial cells and neurons, causing neuronal degeneration [74,75]. Peroxynitrite can further activate NF-κB and B1R, thereby increasing the expression of various pro-inflammatory mediators [76,77]. The activation of B1R can be further amplified and sustained through a positive feedback loop to maintain inflammatory responses and oxidative stress [71]. These studies collectively indicate that the activation of B1R can induce sustained inflammation and oxidative stress in other cells, leading to exacerbation of inflammatory vicious cycles. Most conclusions point to B1R as a ‘bad’ receptor.

B1R promotes expression during tissue damage, while B2R constitutively exists on various cell membranes, participating in numerous physiological regulatory processes. Most of the physiological effects of kinins are mediated by B2R, with BK being a specific agonist for B2R [78]. It is well known that following trauma and infection of peripheral tissues, BK can mediate inflammatory cell infiltration, inflammatory oedema and induce pain. Excessive activation of BK and B2R can lead to pro-inflammatory effects, such as in diseases like vascular neurogenic oedema, septic shock, stroke and hypertension. Antagonizing B2R can have beneficial therapeutic effects, and B2 receptors are crucial for maintaining cardiovascular homeostasis during developmental processes [79]. In this review, we do not discuss the role of B1R; rather, we primarily focus on the relationship between BK acting on B2R and oxidative stress.

A wealth of research has demonstrated that the resistance to oxidative stress by BK–B2R confers cardiorenal protection, possibly mediated through the activation of multiple signalling pathways involving: NO synthase, guanylate cyclase, cyclic dimeric guanosine monophosphate (cGMP), protein kinase G, mitochondrial KATP channels, ROS, kinase C, ERK and Akt, among others, thereby inhibiting oxidative stress, reducing the expression of cellular inflammatory mediators, and apoptosis [80–82]. Inhibiting the degradation of BK can also reduce the risk of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality in cardiovascular patients [83]. In contrast, in vivo studies confirm that inhibiting B2R reduces PI3K/Akt protein concentration in the lower limb venous tissues of thromboangiitis obliterans (TAOs) rats while increasing oxidative stress levels [84]. Similarly, in vivo studies indicate that B2R deficiency leads to increased oxidative stress, mitochondrial damage and endothelial injury, thereby promoting inflammation and apoptosis. This ultimately results in aging, renal fibrosis and kidney injury in diabetic mice [85–87]. It can also lead to microvascular dysfunction, cardiac hypertrophy, heart failure and aging [88–90]. Research has also reported that BK potentiating factor and BK-like peptides extracted from scorpion venom and bee venom can enhance the antioxidant capacity in cellular tissues by activating the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system (RAAS) pathway, mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) pathway, PI3K–AKT pathway, and inhibiting the IκBα–NF-κB signalling pathway, thereby alleviating oxidative stress, reducing apoptosis and halting inflammation, thus mitigating the cardiac, hepatic and renal damage caused by heavy metals and radiation in animals [91–93]. However, these effects can still be blocked by B2R antagonist (HOE140), indicating that they also exert beneficial protective effects by activating downstream pathways through B2R [94–96].

Many studies have confirmed the beneficial effects of the KKS on myocardial ischemia/reperfusion injury, but high levels of BK accelerate aging in the human lung fibroblast line WI-38 and Alzheimer’s disease skin fibroblast lines, while the BK receptor antagonist icatibant demonstrates potent cardiovascular and renal protective effects in a feline model of myocardial ischemia–reperfusion injury [97,98]. BK activation of B2R stimulates NADPH oxidase, uncouples eNOS, activates Ca2+ channels and mediates ROS generation leading to oxidative stress. It can also induce increased production of tumour necrosis factor-α, monocyte chemoattractant protein-1, and upregulation of MMP-9, ultimately resulting in cell apoptosis, inflammation and tissue damage [99–102]. Some studies indicate that the B2R antagonist icatibant can suppress TGF-β overexpression, reduce the activation of extracellular signal-regulated kinase 1/2 and signal transducer and activator of transcription 1 (STAT1), lower ROS levels, and exert anti-inflammatory effects. Consequently, it mitigates the detrimental impact of the KKS on accelerating the progression of DN in mice [103–105].

In terms of mechanism, upon BK binding, B2R activation engages Gβγ to trigger the PI3K/AKT pathway, promoting eNOS phosphorylation at Ser1177 and maintaining eNOS coupling, which increases NO bioavailability and, in concert with the Nrf2 axis, upregulates antioxidant enzymes (SOD, CAT). Under hyperglycaemic or inflammatory conditions, this pathway may intersect with NOX upregulation, and if eNOS becomes uncoupled (e.g. due to BH4 depletion), it produces O2−, amplifying oxidative stress [106]. Meanwhile, low-intensity or chronic stimulation favours extracellular signal-related kinases 1 and 2 (ERK1/2) signalling that supports cell survival and repair and can indirectly promote antioxidative responses, whereas acute high-dose stimulation or inflammatory co-stimulation tends to activate p38/JNK, enhancing NOX expression and pro-inflammatory transcription and shifting the balance towards pro-oxidative outcomes [107]. In addition, studies have shown that after binding BK, B2R can undergo ligand-induced endocytosis and enter the nucleus, where it may act as a transcriptional regulator to modulate gene expression, potentially regulating cellular redox homeostasis at the genetic level [108,109]. It can be seen that the role of BK in oxidative stress is controversial. To facilitate research, we have summarized studies on BK’s involvement in oxidative stress, including the cell types intervened by BK, treatment duration, dosage, and the molecular mechanisms involved in oxidative stress (Table 2).

sec.title: Discussion and conclusions: is it promoting oxidative stress? Or is it antioxidative stress?
sec.p: After analysing the above research data, two main outcomes regarding BK’s involvement in oxidative stress were observed: BK intervention can lead to the generation of ROS, exhibiting harmful effects; however, BK intervention also has a beneficial effect in reducing ROS and antioxidative stress. These two outcomes are controversially opposite. To further explore the potential reasons for the contradictions, we summarized the data from Table 2 (including the types of cells intervened by BK, time, and dosage of intervention) and statistically analysed whether there were any patterns observed. After tallying the aforementioned data, the results indicate that whether BK intervention exerts antioxidative stress does not show a significant correlation with the types of cells and the dosage of BK intervention (Figures 1 and 2).

caption.p: The relationship between BK intervention time and oxidative stress.
The relationship between BK intervention dosage and oxidative stress.

sec.p: However, interestingly, when we conducted indiscriminate statistics on the intervention time and dosage of BK for different cell types, we found that prolonged intervention tended to have a beneficial effect on antioxidative stress, showing significant statistical differences (Figure 3a–c). This result may be limited by the small sample size. However, there are also studies by scholars that reflect this phenomenon. For instance, in the intervention of BK 100 nM on human endothelial cell lines for 30 min, the concentration of NO increased by 55%, while longer incubation for 1 h resulted in a decrease in concentration by approximately 30% [133]. In vivo studies have also indicated that administering BK for only 3 min fails to trigger protective effects on the hearts of ischemic mice, whereas longer interventions yield protective benefits [135]. The mechanism behind this phenomenon may involve an early increase in ROS after BK intervention in cells. The transient elevation of ROS early on may lead researchers to draw harmful conclusions. In essence, the increased ROS can activate MAPK, promoting cell migration and proliferation [136]. This limited amount of ROS can also induce an increase in CAT activity, helping to achieve ischemic tolerance and tissue protection from oxidative stress during reperfusion [137]. A clinical study compared the levels of ROS in peripheral blood mononuclear cells, as well as the plasma levels of advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and advanced oxidation protein products (AOPPs), between 30 patients with hereditary angioedema due to C1 inhibitor deficiency and 15 healthy individuals. It confirmed that the production of an appropriate amount of ROS can increase endogenous antioxidants, prevent free radical damage to the heart during ischemia–reperfusion, and trigger a protective effect [138,139].

caption.p: BK intervention time and dosage relationship with oxidative stress. (a) The relationship between BK intervention time and dosage with oxidative stress without distinguishing cell types; (b) the relationship between BK intervention time and oxidative stress without distinguishing cell types; and (c) the relationship between BK intervention dosage and oxidative stress without distinguishing cell types (*p < 0.05; nsp > 0.05).

sec.p: In summary, short-term, high-dose BK stimulation may generate excessive ROS and drive oxidative stress; in contrast, long-term, low-dose BK intervention may induce small amounts of ROS that activate adaptive antioxidant defences via the B2R pathway. Based on this clear time–dose dependency, when treating certain chronic diseases (such as cardiovascular disease, diabetes and kidney disease) with the goal of inducing antioxidant adaptation, a ‘low-dose, prolonged’ strategy can be prioritized; whereas in contexts with B2R-related high oxidative burden or where further oxidative injury must be avoided, inhibition of this pathway is advisable (e.g. using the B2R antagonist icatibant in angioedema). Future studies should conduct time-resolved pharmacodynamic evaluations and ‘opportunity window’ investigations to systematically delineate the dose–time relationship between BK and oxidative stress, thereby minimizing potential harms and maximizing beneficial effects for disease therapy.
